does: This paper introduces TC-UMIA, a tri-class membership inference attack that evaluates privacy vulnerabilities in machine unlearning beyond the forget set. The attack leverages model predictions before and after unlearning to simultaneously distinguish between forgotten, retained, and unseen data samples at the population level. The authors conduct extensive experiments across five state-of-the-art unlearning algorithms and six real-world datasets, demonstrating that unlearning procedures can paradoxically increase susceptibility of the retain set to membership inference attacks. The work reveals a critical privacy-utility tradeoff in existing unlearning methods: while successfully removing information about forgotten samples, the unlearning process can inadvertently create exploitable patterns in retained data that leak membership information to adversaries.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research examining privacy risks in machine learning, particularly the intersection of membership inference attacks and machine unlearning. It extends prior work that focused primarily on whether forget-set samples could be successfully removed, responding to an important gap where retain-set privacy was largely overlooked. The paper builds on foundational membership inference attack literature while adapting these techniques to the specific unlearning context. It connects to recent work analyzing unlearning effectiveness and fairness, contributing a rigorous privacy evaluation framework. The attack methodology relates to existing population-level inference techniques but innovates by introducing the tri-class classification problem unique to unlearning scenarios where data partitioning matters.
enables: This work enables future research into designing privacy-preserving unlearning algorithms that simultaneously protect both forget and retain sets. It establishes benchmarks and evaluation protocols for assessing unlearning safety, which can inform the development of more robust unlearning methods. The framework opens directions for investigating the theoretical foundations of why unlearning introduces retain-set vulnerabilities and how to mitigate them. The findings highlight the need for privacy accounting in unlearning that goes beyond membership in specific partitions, potentially inspiring new defensive mechanisms or regularization techniques. This research also informs regulatory and practical considerations for deploying machine unlearning in real systems where retain-set privacy is as critical as forget-set privacy.
